domain: wabi-sabi Japanese aesthetic (15th c tea-ceremony principle of imperfect-impermanent-incomplete; valorizes visible flaws — cracks, patina, asymmetry — as meaningful signal of process and time rather than defect; kintsugi gold-fills cracks to celebrate breakage)
specific_mechanism: Imperfection is not noise but MEANINGFUL SIGNAL of provenance: hand-throwing leaves asymmetry; age leaves patina; breakage leaves crack. Cultivated practice valorises these as the aesthetic essence rather than hiding or repairing-invisibly. Kintsugi extends by GILDING repairs, making mended seams more visible.
llm_application: Wabi-sabi calibrated-uncertainty response style: explicitly train the model to leave visible 'cracks' of acknowledged epistemic uncertainty in answers, with per-claim confidence vectors and 'patina annotations' showing which claims came from retrieval / reasoning / training-memory / guess. Visible kintsugi-style 'seams' highlight training-cutoff knowledge gaps. RLHF reward function: polished-but-overconfident answers PENALIZED; visibly-uncertain-but-calibrated answers REWARDED. Aesthetic principle: imperfection is the GOAL of the response style — the assistant is supposed to look hand-thrown, not factory-perfect. Distinct from technical calibration: visible imperfection is celebrated, not minimized.
novelty_claim: LLM uncertainty quantification literature (2506.07461, 2511.00280, 2505.23854) treats imperfection as a cost to MINIMIZE via calibration. Linguistic verbal uncertainty is closest neighbor but is a technical accuracy metric, not an aesthetic principle. The wabi-sabi framing (uncertainty as VALORIZED aesthetic, not minimised technical cost) is conceptually distinct.
